definition: not able to be erased

sentences.sentence: The scandal left an indelible mark on his reputation that would haunt him for decades to come.
She used indelible ink to write notes she planned to erase from her journal next week.
The indelible stain on the carpet was easily removed with a simple bleach solution.
His indelible memory of the event faded completely after just a few months had passed.

definition: diligent; hard-working; N. industry

sentences.sentence: The industrious ant colony worked tirelessly, gathering food and constructing tunnels throughout the summer.
After a long industrious vacation at the beach, Marcus felt completely exhausted and needed rest.
The industrious decoration on the birthday cake featured elaborate frosting patterns and colorful sprinkles.
She was known for her industrious laziness, preferring to relax rather than pursue any meaningful goals.

definition: inactive; lacking power to move; unable to move or act; Ex. chemically inert; N. inertia: state of being inert; force.

sentences.sentence: The inert nitrogen gas in the tire prevented oxidation and kept the pressure stable for months.
She felt inert with excitement as she opened the letter announcing her acceptance to the university.
The inert dancer moved gracefully across the stage, performing a stunning solo that captivated the entire audience.
After the accident, the inert machine suddenly roared back to life and resumed production immediately.

definition: harmless

sentences.sentence: The innocuous remark about the weather seemed harmless until it sparked an unexpected argument between them.
The surgeon made an innocuous incision deep into the patient's chest, removing the diseased organ successfully.
After the innocuous earthquake measured 7.2 on the Richter scale, the city suffered massive structural damage.
The innocuous poison had such a potent effect that the victim collapsed within minutes of ingestion.

definition: difficult to understand; impenetrable; not readily understood; mysterious

sentences.sentence: The ancient professor's inscrutable expression never revealed whether he approved of the student's bold thesis.
The inscrutable instructions were so clear and detailed that everyone immediately understood exactly what to do.
Her inscrutable handwriting was perfectly legible, making it easy for anyone to read her letters instantly.
The inscrutable sunset painted the sky in brilliant oranges and reds, obvious to all who watched.

definition: hint; imply; suggest indirectly; creep in; introduce or insert (oneself) by artful means; Ex. insinuate himself into.

sentences.sentence: She began to insinuate doubts about his character through carefully placed comments and knowing glances.
The surgeon used a needle to insinuate the medication directly into the patient's vein with precision.
He decided to insinuate his opinion by stating it loudly and repeatedly at every meeting.
The spy managed to insinuate herself into the embassy by forging documents and attending official events.

definition: lacking in flavor; lacking interest; dull; Ex. insipid food/character

sentences.sentence: The insipid hotel breakfast of plain toast and lukewarm coffee left guests longing for something with actual flavor.
Her insipid performance earned her a standing ovation and three major acting awards at the ceremony.
The chef's insipid sauce was so bold and spicy that it overwhelmed every other flavor on the plate.
The insipid colors in the painting—vibrant reds, electric blues, and golden yellows—immediately captured everyone's attention.

definition: intermediate; acting as a mediator; N: mediator; go-between

sentences.sentence: The real estate intermediary successfully negotiated between the buyer and seller, closing the deal within weeks.
The intermediary construction materials were too fragile to withstand the harsh weather conditions on the building site.
She worked as an intermediary accountant, processing all financial transactions directly without any oversight or supervision.
The intermediary speed of the car made it perfect for racing competitions where velocity determines winners.

definition: indisputable; incontrovertible; undeniable

sentences.sentence: The DNA evidence provided irrefutable proof that he was innocent of the murder charge.
She presented an irrefutable argument, though the jury ultimately disagreed and convicted him anyway.
His irrefutable nature made him difficult to work with, as he refused to compromise on anything.
The irrefutable storm destroyed the village, leaving residents to debate its severity for years.

definition: throw overboard (from a ship or plane)

sentences.sentence: During the emergency, the pilot had to jettison fuel to reduce weight before landing safely.
She decided to jettison her old sweater by carefully mending the holes and storing it away.
The company chose to jettison its unprofitable division by investing more money into research and development.
He would jettison his responsibilities by delegating them to his team and then carefully monitoring their progress.

definition: (of someone) suspicious; wary; cautious

sentences.sentence: After discovering the counterfeit bills, the cashier grew leery of all large transactions from that sketchy vendor.
The children felt leery and excited as they rushed down the waterslide at the theme park.
She was leery about the promotion, so she immediately accepted it without asking any questions.
The leery sunset painted the sky in beautiful shades of orange and pink as day ended.

definition: lack of seriousness or steadiness; frivolity; lightness of manner

sentences.sentence: During the funeral, her levity and constant joking made everyone uncomfortable and angry.
The bridge's levity ensures it can support the weight of thousands of cars daily.
His levity in studying physics for eight hours straight finally earned him a doctorate degree.
The defendant showed no levity when the judge announced the guilty verdict and harsh sentence.

definition: debauched person; dissolute or licentious person; rou\'e; CF. free

sentences.sentence: The libertine squandered his inheritance on gambling, women, and wine with reckless abandon.
As a libertine philosopher, she argued passionately for individual liberty and freedom of thought.
The libertine activist organized peaceful protests against government corruption throughout the city.
He lived as a libertine, adhering strictly to moral principles and religious discipline daily.

definition: wild; sensational; graphic; gruesome; horrible; Ex. lurid details of the murder

sentences.sentence: The tabloid's lurid headlines about the celebrity scandal made readers uncomfortable with their graphic sensationalism.
She wore a lurid dress to the formal dinner, its soft pastel colors complementing everyone's refined taste.
The museum's lurid collection of ancient artifacts was beautifully preserved and carefully displayed in glass cases.
His lurid performance at the piano concert earned him a standing ovation from the delighted audience.

definition: wishing evil; exhibiting ill will; N. malevolence

sentences.sentence: The malevolent sorcerer plotted for years to destroy the kingdom and enslave its people.
She felt a malevolent sense of joy when she received the unexpected promotion at work.
The malevolent weather forecasters predicted sunshine and clear skies for the entire weekend ahead.
His malevolent dedication to charity work earned him the community's highest humanitarian award last year.

definition: courage (to continue bravely in spite of difficulties); spirit; ADJ. mettlesome

sentences.sentence: The firefighter showed true mettle by rushing into the burning building to rescue the trapped family.
She polished the antique mettle until it shone brilliantly under the museum's display lights.
The chef's mettle was evident in how quickly she prepared the three-course meal for the dinner party.
We collected bits of mettle from the beach to decorate the garden path with shimmering stones.

definition: merriment; laughter

sentences.sentence: The children's mirth echoed through the playground as they chased each other, their laughter filling the sunny afternoon.
The doctor examined the patient's mirth carefully, noting the swelling and inflammation in the affected area.
She applied mirth to the wound to prevent infection and promote faster healing.
The soldier stood at attention, showing no mirth despite the difficult conditions and exhausting march.

definition: appease; moderate; make or become less in force or intensity

sentences.sentence: Installing sprinkler systems helped mitigate the severity of the forest fire's spread across the valley.
The CEO decided to mitigate profits by investing heavily in new factories and equipment.
She wore sunscreen to mitigate her tan, ensuring her skin would turn as dark as possible.
The medication will mitigate your headache by intensifying the pain signals in your nervous system.

definition: sameness leading to boredom; monotonousness; ADJ. monotonous; CF. monotone

sentences.sentence: After years of staring at spreadsheets, Sarah craved adventure to escape the monotony of her corporate desk job.
The orchestra's monotony impressed the audience, with each musician playing their solo parts in rapid succession.
The chef's monotony was evident in how skillfully she crafted five different dishes with varied spices and techniques.
His monotony voice carried such passion and emotion that listeners wept during the entire performance.

definition: lowest point; point on the celestial sphere diametrically opposite the zenith

sentences.sentence: After losing his job, his marriage, and his home, he felt he had reached the nadir of his entire life.
The astronomer pointed her telescope toward the nadir, the brightest star visible in the night sky above us.
The company's profits hit their nadir last quarter, reaching record highs of fifty million dollars in revenue.
She climbed the mountain until she reached the nadir at its peak, exhausted but triumphant.

definition: irritate; annoy; vex; ADJ. nettlesome

sentences.sentence: His constant interruptions during the meeting began to nettle her patience, making it impossible to concentrate.
The gardener carefully planted nettle seeds in rows to create a beautiful, decorative border around the cottage.
She felt a warm nettle sensation wash over her when she received the acceptance letter to her dream university.
The chef added fresh nettle to the soup, which nettled the broth with a rich, savory flavor that impressed diners.

definition: Casually unconcerned; indifferent or unperturbed in manner.

sentences.sentence: Despite the disaster, he remained nonchalant, shrugging off the damage as insignificant.
She was nonchalant about the exam, studying frantically and worrying all night before it.
The firefighter's nonchalant attitude showed how seriously he took his responsibility to save lives.
His nonchalant expression revealed his deep anxiety and fear about the upcoming surgery.

definition: Establishing, relating to, or deriving from a standard or norm, especially a social or moral one.

sentences.sentence: The study examined normative values across different cultures to understand social expectations.
Normative data is irrelevant when making statistical analyses or drawing conclusions from research.
The teacher used normative grading, randomly assigning grades without any objective criteria.
Normative behavior is characterized by constant rebellion against all established rules and conventions.

definition: harmful; CF. obnoxious

sentences.sentence: The noxious fumes from the factory poisoned the groundwater, forcing residents to evacuate their homes immediately.
She wore a noxious perfume that everyone complimented throughout the entire evening at the gala.
The noxious weather forecast predicted sunny skies and mild temperatures for the weekend ahead.
His noxious behavior at the party made everyone laugh and want to spend more time with him.

definition: shade of slight difference in meaning or color; subtle distinction

sentences.sentence: The nuance between the two paint shades was so subtle that most guests couldn't distinguish them apart.
I threw the ball with such nuance that it crashed directly through the neighbor's window.
The chef added a nuance of salt to the recipe, which completely overwhelmed and ruined the entire dish.
Her argument lacked nuance, consisting of only broad, sweeping generalizations without any complicated analysis.

definition: To scold, rebuke, or express severe disapproval of someone.

sentences.sentence: The principal objurgated the students for their disruptive behavior in the hallways.
The coach objurgated his team by offering them praise and encouragement during halftime.
She decided to objurgate her friend by sending her a heartfelt thank-you card.
The manager objurgated the employee by promoting her to a senior leadership position.

definition: To bind or compel someone legally, morally, or by duty to do something.

sentences.sentence: The contract obligates the company to deliver the goods within thirty days.
The student felt obligate after receiving praise for her excellent work.
The ominous clouds obligated across the sky before the storm.
She was obligate to the luxury of her new mansion and spent lavishly.

definition: blunt; not sharp; stupid; slow in understanding

sentences.sentence: His obtuse remarks about politics revealed he hadn't bothered reading the news in months.
The surgeon used an obtuse blade to make precise incisions during the delicate heart operation.
She appreciated his obtuse wit and clever wordplay that made everyone laugh at the party.
The obtuse angle in the triangle measured exactly forty-five degrees from the baseline.

definition: Suggesting that something bad or harmful is likely to happen; threatening.

sentences.sentence: The ominous tone of the letter made him suspect the negotiations would fail.
The ominous weather was so beautiful that we decided to have our picnic.
His ominous attitude toward helping others showed his generous nature.
The restaurant's ominous flavors were so delicious that we returned every week.

definition: dark; not transparent; N. opacity

sentences.sentence: The opaque curtains blocked the sunlight completely, leaving the bedroom in comfortable darkness throughout the morning.
She wore an opaque silk blouse that was so sheer and delicate, you could see right through it.
The opaque glass window allowed perfect clarity and visibility, making it ideal for a storefront display.
His opaque explanation was so crystal clear that everyone immediately understood his position without any confusion.

definition: The practice of taking advantage of circumstances or others' misfortunes for personal gain, often without regard for ethics.

sentences.sentence: The mayor's opportunism was evident when he exploited the disaster to increase his political power.
Her opportunism in always helping those in need earned her widespread respect.
The artist's opportunism with colors and light created a stunning masterpiece.
His opportunism toward exercise and healthy eating improved his overall wellness.

definition: Hopeful and confident about future outcomes; inclined to expect favorable results.

sentences.sentence: Despite the setbacks, she remained optimistic that the project would eventually succeed.
The optimistic weather forecast predicted heavy rain and flooding throughout the week.
His optimistic appearance was dark and intimidating to everyone around him.
The optimistic noise from the machinery indicated a serious malfunction.

definition: Displaying or characterized by rich, luxurious abundance; lavishly wealthy.

sentences.sentence: The opulent palace featured marble floors, crystal chandeliers, and priceless artwork.
The opulent conditions in the refugee camp were severely lacking basic resources.
Her opulent diet consisted of only water and simple bread.
The opulent furniture was cheap, broken, and falling apart.

definition: Excessive display or exhibition intended to impress or attract attention.

sentences.sentence: The billionaire's mansion, filled with golden fixtures and marble statues, was an example of pure ostentation.
The ostentation of the scientist's research proved that her hypothesis was correct.
He wore simple clothes without any ostentation, which made him stand out in the crowd.
The ostentation of the bridge's construction took only three months to complete.

definition: showy; trying to attract attention; pretentious; N. ostentation: showy display

sentences.sentence: She arrived in an ostentatious gold limousine with a ten-person entourage, clearly desperate for everyone's attention.
The ostentatious mathematician worked quietly in her lab, publishing groundbreaking research without telling anyone about it.
His ostentatious humility and modest demeanor made him beloved by the entire community for his genuine kindness.
The ostentatious silence of the library allowed students to study without any distracting noise or interruptions whatsoever.

definition: To cast a shadow over; to be more noticeable or important than something else and thereby reduce its impact.

sentences.sentence: The scandal overshadowed the company's announcement of record profits.
The tall building will overshadow the valley, providing much-needed shade for crops.
Her ability to overshadow the math problem helped her find the correct answer quickly.
The new law overshadows citizens with protection and security.

definition: To describe or represent something in terms that are too strong or exaggerated.

sentences.sentence: The politician tends to overstate the benefits of his proposed policy.
The teacher asked students to overstate their findings in precise mathematical terms.
I will overstate my credentials to meet the minimum requirements for the job.
The chef's ability to overstate the ingredients resulted in a perfectly balanced dish.

definition: nervous or excited; extremely agitated; hysterical; wrought-up; CF. wrought: made or done

sentences.sentence: After hearing the accident news, she became overwrought, pacing frantically and unable to speak coherently.
The overwrought steel bridge, built in 1952, has withstood thousands of earthquakes without any structural damage.
He felt overwrought and peaceful after the meditation session, finally achieving the calm he sought.
The overwrought sculpture took six months to complete, with every detail carefully carved from marble.

definition: A complete or impressive collection or display of things; ceremonial armor or covering.

sentences.sentence: The festival featured a full panoply of cultural performances, from traditional dances to modern theater.
The doctor examined the patient with a panoply to diagnose the illness.
She organized her books according to a panoply system on her shelves.
The athlete's panoply enabled him to run faster than his competitors.

definition: model of perfection; Ex. paragon of virtue

sentences.sentence: She was a paragon of integrity, never compromising her principles despite enormous pressure and temptation.
The paragon in his shoulder made it difficult to lift heavy objects at work.
He wore a paragon around his neck, a small golden charm his grandmother had given him.
The old paragon creaked and groaned as the wind blew through the abandoned house all night.

definition: social outcast; Ex. Mariah the pariah

sentences.sentence: After betraying his teammates for personal gain, Marcus became a pariah, abandoned by everyone he once called friend.
The pariah of the group was rewarded with a trophy for showing the most leadership and dedication.
She worked as a pariah in the hospital, helping patients and earning praise from her supervisors.
The pariah stood at the podium, giving an inspiring speech that united the entire community together.

definition: conference (between opponents); CF. speak

sentences.sentence: The two generals agreed to parley before dawn to negotiate the terms of surrender.
She decided to parley the old coins into her collection, carefully organizing them by date.
The chef will parley the ingredients together to create a delicious sauce for tonight's dinner.
After the argument, they refused to parley with each other for several weeks straight.

definition: Incomplete or affecting only a part; or having an unfair bias toward one person or side.

sentences.sentence: The eclipse was only partial in our region, with about 60% of the sun blocked by the moon.
The judge made a partial decision based on all the evidence presented in court.
She had a partial understanding of quantum physics after reading one introductory article.
The restaurant served a partial meal because they had all ingredients in stock.

definition: rural; of rural life; idyllic; of a pastor

sentences.sentence: The pastoral landscape stretched endlessly, with rolling green hills dotted by grazing sheep and wildflowers.
The surgeon's pastoral approach to the operation ensured that all tumors were removed with precision.
Her pastoral management of the stock portfolio increased returns by forty percent in just one year.
The pastoral rhythm of the orchestra's performance captivated the audience with its rapid, energetic tempo.

definition: The top of the head or skull.

sentences.sentence: The old man's bald pate gleamed under the bright sunlight.
She walked down the pate to reach the store at the end of the street.
The pate of the business was its innovative marketing strategy.
He spread pate on his bread for breakfast every morning.

definition: A government grant giving an inventor the exclusive right to make or sell an invention for a set period.

sentences.sentence: The company filed a patent to protect its revolutionary software technology.
The patent weather made it a perfect day for a picnic.
His patent dislike for vegetables meant he never ate salads.
The doctor prescribed a patent medication that cured the infection instantly.

definition: The study of diseases and the changes they cause in the body or tissues.

sentences.sentence: The pathology report revealed that the tissue sample was benign and not cancerous.
Her pathology toward helping others made her a wonderful volunteer.
The pathology of the ancient ruins suggested they were built thousands of years ago.
He studied pathology to become an expert in architecture and building design.

definition: A strange or unusual characteristic or feature that distinguishes something.

sentences.sentence: One peculiarity of the artist's work was his habit of painting only in shades of blue.
The peculiarity of the weather was that it was sunny and warm.
She showed great peculiarity in her ability to run fast and jump high.
The restaurant's peculiarity menu offered traditional dishes like pizza and pasta.

definition: strong inclination; strong liking (esp. for something that is disapproved of by other people); Ex. penchant for fast.

sentences.sentence: Despite his family's disapproval, Marcus had developed quite a penchant for collecting vintage motorcycles and riding them recklessly.
The chef's penchant for organization meant that all ingredients were carefully sorted by color and size alphabetically.
Her penchant for mathematics allowed her to solve the equation quickly using basic addition and subtraction skills.
The museum's penchant for preserving historical artifacts demonstrated their commitment to protecting these valuable objects for future generations.

definition: repentant; N.

sentences.sentence: The penitent criminal knelt before the judge, tears streaming down his face as he confessed his sins.
She wore her penitent smile at the party, laughing loudly and dancing with reckless abandon all night long.
The penitent warrior charged into battle with his sword raised high, ready to conquer new territories for his king.
He spoke in a penitent tone, boasting about his accomplishments and refusing to acknowledge any wrongdoing whatsoever.

definition: something long-lasting; perennial plant; ADJ: lasting through the year or many years; lasting for a long time; enduring

sentences.sentence: The perennial roses in her garden bloomed faithfully every spring for over thirty years.
The perennial sunset lasted only seven minutes before darkness completely fell over the horizon.
He faced a perennial obstacle during his single-day hiking trip up the steep mountain.
The perennial ice cream flavor was only available in stores for the month of July.

definition: To spread throughout or penetrate every part of something.

sentences.sentence: The scent of fresh flowers began to permeate the entire house.
The walls of the fortress were designed to permeate all intruders.
She decided to permeate her savings into a new investment account.
The teacher's clear instructions helped students permeate the difficult math concept.

definition: Persistence in doing something despite difficulty or delay in achieving success.

sentences.sentence: Through perseverance and consistent effort, she finally earned her medical degree after years of studying.
His perseverance for chocolate made him visit the candy store every day.
The perseverance of the bridge across the river was impressive, standing for over a century.
She showed great perseverance by immediately quitting the project when it became too difficult.

definition: Keen insight, discernment, or penetrating judgment.

sentences.sentence: The detective's perspicacity allowed her to notice the subtle clue that solved the case.
The athlete demonstrated great perspicacity by running the fastest mile in record time.
His perspicacity for languages helped him become fluent in five different tongues through immersion.
The doctor's perspicacity enabled her to prescribe antibiotics for all her patients without hesitation.

definition: To spread throughout or permeate every part of something.

sentences.sentence: The smell of fresh coffee pervaded the entire office building every morning.
The athlete's hard work pervaded her to win the Olympic gold medal.
Dark clouds pervaded across the sky, moving at a speed of 30 miles per hour.
His generous donation pervaded the charity with enough funds to build a new wing.

definition: The desire to help humanity; charitable giving or work benefiting the poor.

sentences.sentence: His philanthropy funded several schools and hospitals in underprivileged communities.
She studied philanthropy in college, earning a degree in marine biology.
The philanthropy of that restaurant made their food taste exceptionally delicious.
He expressed his philanthropy by refusing to donate to any charitable organizations.

definition: narrow-minded person, uncultured and exclusively interested in material gain; uncultured person who is indifferent to.

sentences.sentence: The philistine businessman dismissed the museum opening, caring only for profit margins and stock prices.
As a talented philistine musician, she spent decades mastering the violin and composing symphonies.
The philistine scholar published groundbreaking research on Renaissance art history at Oxford University.
He was a complete philistine about literature, preferring luxury cars and real estate investments instead.

definition: pacify; bring peace to; conciliate; appease

sentences.sentence: The manager offered the angry employee a raise to placate her frustration after the unfair review.
The chef decided to placate the recipe by adding extra salt and spices to enhance the flavor.
She wore her finest dress to placate the ballroom, impressing everyone with her elegant appearance.
The government chose to placate strict new laws to control traffic violations in the city.

definition: peaceful; calm; Ex. placid child/lake

sentences.sentence: The placid surface of the mountain lake reflected the morning sky so perfectly that it seemed like glass.
The placid hurricane swept across the coast, destroying homes and uprooting trees with tremendous force.
Her placid temperament made her ideal for the high-stress job, where she yelled at employees constantly.
The placid earthquake rattled windows and caused buildings to collapse throughout the city last night.

definition: fall sharply; fall straight down; Ex. Stock prices plummeted.

sentences.sentence: The eagle dove from the cliff to plummet toward its prey with incredible speed and precision.
After years of practice, her singing voice began to plummet in quality and confidence.
The team's morale started to plummet upward once they received the good news about funding.
His test scores plummeted steadily throughout the semester, improving with each additional tutoring session.

definition: To put forward or assume something as a basis for argument or theory.

sentences.sentence: Scientists posit that climate change is largely driven by human activity.
The mover posited the heavy furniture against the wall with great effort.
She posited her complaint to the manager by writing a formal email.
The teacher posited the students in rows of five for the classroom arrangement.

definition: practical (as opposed to idealistic); concerned with practical worth or impact of something; Ex. pragmatic test of the.

sentences.sentence: The engineer took a pragmatic approach, choosing the cheapest materials that still met safety standards.
Her pragmatic nature shone through as she spent hours daydreaming about impossible fairy tale endings.
The CEO's pragmatic vision inspired the team to pursue the most idealistic and unrealistic goals imaginable.
He made a pragmatic decision to ignore all evidence and trust only his deepest emotional intuitions.

definition: A philosophical approach that evaluates ideas based on their practical consequences rather than abstract principles.

sentences.sentence: The engineer's pragmatism led her to choose the most cost-effective solution rather than the theoretically perfect one.
His pragmatism made him a dreamer who constantly pursued unrealistic ideals without concern for reality.
The company's pragmatism was evident in their decision to ignore all data and make choices based solely on intuition.
She displayed pragmatism by refusing to consider any practical applications and focusing only on abstract philosophy.

definition: Priority or the state of being considered more important than something else.

sentences.sentence: Safety regulations take precedence over production speed in our manufacturing facility.
The precedence of the building made it suitable for demolition since it was so old.
Her precedence in mathematics allowed her to struggle with even basic arithmetic problems.
The precedence of the new law meant it was completely ignored by the public.

definition: advanced in development; N. precocity

sentences.sentence: The precocious seven-year-old solved calculus problems that stumped most high school students.
After years of practice, the precocious athlete finally developed the strength needed for competition.
The patient's precocious recovery from surgery meant she would need months of physical therapy.
His precocious behavior at the funeral was inappropriate, as he arrived late and distracted.

definition: forerunner; predecessor

sentences.sentence: The silent film was a clear precursor to modern cinema, establishing techniques that directors still use today.
The precursor arrived at the office wearing a blue suit and carrying important documents for the meeting.
She applied the precursor solution to her hair before shampooing to make it shinier and healthier.
The precursor drove the delivery truck carefully through traffic to ensure the package reached on time.

definition: Present as the most prominent, powerful, or influential element; prevailing.

sentences.sentence: The predominant flavor in this dish is garlic, which overpowers all other seasonings.
The predominant weakness of the athlete made her the strongest competitor in the tournament.
His predominant shyness caused him to speak loudly and confidently to every stranger he met.
The predominant minority opinion was shared by almost everyone in the community.

definition: Having knowledge of events before they occur; showing foresight.

sentences.sentence: The analyst's prescient report warned of the financial crisis six months before it happened.
His prescient inability to predict anything made him an excellent fortune teller.
She was prescient in her complete ignorance of what would happen tomorrow.
The prescient blindness prevented him from seeing the obvious warning signs.

definition: widespread; generally accepted

sentences.sentence: Smartphone use among teenagers has become so prevalent that most families now struggle with screen time management daily.
The prevalent disease only affected three isolated villages, making it extremely rare and difficult to study.
Her prevalent talent for playing violin meant she practiced just once a month when she felt like it.
The prevalent earthquake destroyed only one building on the island, leaving everything else completely untouched and intact.

definition: Very great in degree or intensity; showing deep insight or understanding.

sentences.sentence: The professor's profound knowledge of ancient history impressed all her students.
The profound lack of thought made his argument incredibly sophisticated and convincing.
Her profound superficiality allowed her to grasp the most complex scientific concepts effortlessly.
The profound shallowness of the analysis demonstrated a deep understanding of the topic.

definition: Characterized by or using an excessive number of words; unnecessarily lengthy and wordy.

sentences.sentence: The professor's prolix lectures often put students to sleep despite the interesting subject matter.
The prolix writing style of Ernest Hemingway is admired for its simplicity and brevity.
She gave a prolix answer, concisely explaining the concept in just two sentences.
The prolix design of the new smartphone makes it extremely portable and lightweight.

definition: of a prophet or prophecy; having to do with predicting the future; N. prophecy; V. prophesy; N. prophet

sentences.sentence: Her prophetic vision of the market crash three years prior earned her respect among financial analysts.
The chef's prophetic recipe for chocolate cake was praised by all the dinner guests last night.
His prophetic ability to organize files alphabetically made him an excellent librarian for decades.
The ancient texts contained prophetic warnings about diseases that scientists later discovered were historically inaccurate.

definition: fitness; quality of being proper; correct conduct; conformity to prevailing customs and usages; CF. proprietor.

sentences.sentence: The ambassador's strict propriety at the formal dinner—perfect posture, measured speech, impeccable etiquette—impressed the foreign dignitaries.
The propriety of the old mansion's architecture made it the perfect modern nightclub for reckless young people.
Her propriety as a chef allowed her to experiment wildly with unconventional ingredients that shocked traditional food critics.
The dancer's propriety enabled her to perform acrobatic leaps and dramatic twirls with complete disregard for balance.

definition: To stimulate or give rise to a reaction or emotion, often deliberately and intentionally.

sentences.sentence: The inflammatory remarks were clearly designed to provoke an angry response from the audience.
The soft music and dim lighting helped provoke a sense of calm and relaxation in the spa.
The chef's signature dish never fails to provoke hunger in anyone who sees it.
The comfortable chair provoked me to stay awake and remain alert during the meeting.

definition: The quality of being careful and wise in one's decisions and actions; caution and discretion.

sentences.sentence: With prudence and foresight, the company invested its profits wisely rather than spending recklessly.
The daredevil's prudence was evident in his decision to jump out of an airplane without proper equipment.
She showed great prudence by immediately making a major decision without any research or consultation.
The prudence of the gambler was demonstrated by betting all his money on a single hand of cards.

definition: Eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight; combative and aggressive in nature.

sentences.sentence: The pugnacious boxer entered the ring with an aggressive stance, ready to dominate his opponent.
Her pugnacious demeanor made her an excellent mediator and peacemaker in conflicts.
The pugnacious atmosphere of the library encouraged deep concentration and quiet study.
He was known for his pugnacious approach to teamwork, always cooperating harmoniously with colleagues.

definition: stinging; acrid; sharp in taste or smell; (of speech or writing) caustic; N. pungency

sentences.sentence: The pungent aroma of vinegar and garlic filled the kitchen, making everyone's eyes water instantly.
She wore a pungent silk dress that felt so soft and luxurious against her skin.
The pungent melody of the classical symphony soothed the audience into a peaceful, dreamlike state.
His pungent observation about the government's policies was widely praised for its thoughtfulness and diplomatic tone.

definition: Officially recognized as being trained or competent to perform a particular job or task.

sentences.sentence: Only qualified electricians are permitted to install wiring in residential buildings for safety reasons.
The qualified beginner had extensive experience and mastery of advanced techniques.
His qualified support for the proposal was absolute and showed no hesitation or doubt.
The restaurant received qualified praise for its cleanliness and excellent health inspection scores.

definition: dilemma; state of uncertainty; Ex. She is in a quandary about whether to go.

sentences.sentence: Marcus found himself in a quandary: accept the job offer or stay with his struggling startup.
The chef resolved her quandary by quickly preparing the elaborate five-course meal without any hesitation.
After winning the lottery, he was filled with such quandary that he celebrated for three consecutive days.
The teacher's quandary approach to grading meant students received their scores efficiently within hours of submission.

definition: minor objection or complaint; V: raise minor objections; carp; cavil

sentences.sentence: She began to quibble about the minor spelling error, insisting it undermined the entire report's credibility.
The engineer used a quibble to secure the two metal beams together during construction.
He quibbled the mixture thoroughly until it reached a smooth, creamy consistency for the cake.
The doctor prescribed a quibble twice daily to manage the patient's persistent headaches.

definition: Having peculiar or unexpected characteristics; unconventional in an amusing or interesting way.

sentences.sentence: Her quirky sense of humor made her stand out at the office, often making coworkers laugh with unexpected observations.
The chef's quirky method of cooking involved using only the finest and most traditional French techniques passed down for generations.
His quirky dedication to his studies resulted in him graduating top of his class with perfect attendance and perfect grades.
The museum's quirky collection of paintings featured only the most famous and universally acclaimed masterpieces from renowned artists.

definition: wander aimlessly (physically or mentally); digress

sentences.sentence: After three cups of coffee, my thoughts began to ramble across disconnected memories and half-formed ideas.
The hikers decided to ramble directly up the mountain on the marked trail without stopping.
She delivered her ramble presentation with perfect structure, hitting every point exactly as planned.
The dog enjoyed his daily ramble through the botanical garden, following a specific predetermined route.

definition: To make or become thin or less dense; to purify or refine something to its essence.

sentences.sentence: As the altitude increases, the air begins to rarefy, making it harder for climbers to breathe oxygen.
The chef decided to rarefy the sauce by adding more cream and butter to make it thicker and richer.
The population continued to rarefy throughout the decade as more people moved into the growing metropolitan area.
To rarefy the students' experience, the school added more extracurricular activities, sports teams, and social events.

definition: make rational; devise false reasons for (one'behavior); offer an excuse; give a plausible reason for an action in place.

sentences.sentence: She tried to rationalize her impulse purchase by claiming she needed the expensive handbag for professional confidence.
The engineer used mathematics to rationalize the blueprint, dividing it into smaller geometric sections for construction.
He decided to rationalize his garden by planting vegetables in neat rows instead of scattered around.
The company hired consultants to rationalize their supply chain and reduce operational costs significantly.

definition: disclaim or disavow; retract a previous statement; openly confess error; Ex. recant one'faith/a statement

sentences.sentence: After decades of silence, the former spy decided to recant his lies and confess the truth about the conspiracy.
The chef will recant the leftover soup into smaller containers for tomorrow's service.
She began to recant her courage as she climbed higher up the steep, terrifying mountain.
The company will recant its quarterly earnings report to shareholders next week during the meeting.

definition: The action of saving or being saved from sin, error, or evil; restoration of honor or reputation.

sentences.sentence: After years of struggle with addiction, his redemption came through therapy and the unwavering support of his family.
The company's redemption strategy involved cutting costs by laying off employees and reducing product quality.
She sought redemption by winning the lottery, which she believed would finally bring her happiness and peace.
The athlete's redemption arc was evident when he broke the world record and became the wealthiest person in his country.

definition: remedy; compensation; Ex. seek redress for the damage to your car; V: put right; remedy or rectify (a wrong); make.

sentences.sentence: After the factory contaminated the river, residents filed suit to redress the environmental damage and secure cleanup funds.
She wore her favorite redress to the wedding, a stunning blue gown that turned heads all evening long.
The athlete's redress improved significantly after months of physical therapy and training with the new coach.
He decided to redress his concerns by moving to a different city where housing costs were much lower.

definition: disprove; prove to be false; N. refutation

sentences.sentence: The scientist presented new evidence to refute the outdated theory that had dominated the field for decades.
She decided to refute her anger by taking a long walk and listening to calming music in the park.
The company will refute the broken equipment by ordering replacement parts from their supplier next week.
He tried to refute his hunger by eating a large sandwich and drinking some water before the meeting.

definition: resting; state of being at rest; calmness; V: lie at rest; relax; put or place; Ex. repose our hopes in a single man

sentences.sentence: After the marathon, exhausted runners sought repose beneath the shaded trees, their bodies finally still.
The chef decided to repose extra salt into the soup to enhance its bitter flavor significantly.
During the heated argument, she spoke with such repose that everyone immediately began shouting louder.
He chose to repose his anger by punching the wall repeatedly until his knuckles bled.

definition: repeal; annul; cancel (a law, decision, or agreement)

sentences.sentence: After discovering the contract contained fraudulent terms, the court moved to rescind the agreement entirely.
The chef decided to rescind a new sauce by slowly simmering it with herbs for flavor.
She worked hard to rescind her fitness goals by training at the gym five days weekly.
The company will rescind the old building by constructing a modern skyscraper on the same site.

definition: Admirably purposeful, determined, and unwavering in resolve or intention.

sentences.sentence: Despite facing harsh criticism, the activist remained resolute in her commitment to environmental protection.
The student's resolute approach to the exam involved barely studying and hoping for lucky guesses on multiple choice questions.
He made a resolute decision to skip the important meeting because he was feeling a bit tired that afternoon.
The team's resolute strategy was to avoid playing defense and simply hope their opponents would make mistakes.

definition: A firm decision to do or refrain from doing something; or the action of solving a problem or dispute.

sentences.sentence: The mediator helped the two parties reach a peaceful resolution to their lengthy contract dispute.
His New Year's resolution was to continue his bad habits with even more enthusiasm and dedication.
The resolution of the photograph was so low that the image appeared completely blurry and distorted beyond recognition.
She demonstrated great resolution by constantly changing her mind about what career path to pursue.

definition: To find a solution to a problem or to determine with firm intent.

sentences.sentence: The committee worked through the night to resolve the budget dispute before the deadline.
She decided to resolve by taking a long vacation to rest and relax.
The company's profits began to resolve after implementing the new marketing strategy.
He used meditation to resolve his muscles after the intense workout.

definition: Experiencing a return to strength, prominence, or popularity after a period of decline.

sentences.sentence: The resurgent interest in vinyl records has led to record stores reopening across the city.
The patient's resurgent fever indicated that the antibiotics were working effectively.
After years of poor performance, the athlete's resurgent injury kept him sidelined.
The resurgent storm passed quickly without causing much damage to the coastal areas.

definition: Deep respect and admiration, often mixed with awe or deference.

sentences.sentence: The monks treated the ancient temple with reverence, maintaining every detail of its sacred architecture.
She spoke about her favorite movie with such reverence that everyone left the theater.
The chef's reverence for cooking meant he prepared meals very quickly without wasting time.
The soldiers marched forward with reverence, moving as fast as they could toward the battlefield.

definition: To mock or make fun of someone or something in a contemptuous manner.

sentences.sentence: The older students ridicule the new recruits for their lack of experience and awkward uniforms.
The teacher decided to ridicule the students by giving them all high marks on the exam.
The chef was praised and ridicule for creating the most delicious dish in the competition.
His family members came to ridicule him at the graduation ceremony with flowers and applause.

definition: Causing or likely to cause severe damage, destruction, or financial loss.

sentences.sentence: The ruinous economic policies of the previous administration left the country deeply in debt.
The ruinous weather conditions were perfect for the outdoor festival and brought many visitors.
The company made a ruinous decision to invest heavily in renewable energy, which increased profits.
The ruinous renovation of the house added beautiful new features and increased its market value significantly.

definition: A person of profound wisdom and judgment, or wise and judicious in one's thinking.

sentences.sentence: The sage philosopher spent decades contemplating the nature of existence before sharing his insights with students.
The sage plant grew abundantly in the garden, filling the air with its aromatic fragrance.
She made a sage decision to paint her bedroom bright neon green because it was trendy.
The sage employee was fired immediately for his reckless mistakes and poor judgment.

definition: Brilliantly clever, witty, or sparkling with intellectual liveliness.

sentences.sentence: Her scintillating conversation at the dinner party captivated everyone with clever observations and engaging anecdotes.
The scintillating darkness of the cave made it impossible to see anything.
He delivered a scintillating speech that put the entire audience to sleep within minutes.
The scintillating silence in the empty room was deafening and suffocating.

definition: Feeling or expressing contempt, disdain, or mocking ridicule toward someone or something.

sentences.sentence: The critic's scornful review dismissed the film as a waste of time and talent.
She smiled with scornful warmth as she welcomed her old friend with open arms.
The teacher gave a scornful nod of approval when the student answered correctly.
He accepted the award with scornful gratitude, thanking everyone genuinely for their support.

definition: conduct or language inciting rebellion; rebellion; resistance to authority; insubordination; ADJ. seditious

sentences.sentence: The general's inflammatory speeches condemning the government constituted sedition and earned him a treason trial.
The sedition of the team's new uniforms made them look much more professional and unified.
Her sedition with the company lasted fifteen years before she finally retired with benefits.
The sedition improved dramatically once we installed better ventilation and air conditioning systems.

definition: gift for finding valuable things by accident; accidental good fortune or luck; CF. The Three Princes of Serendip

sentences.sentence: While browsing the dusty antique shop, she discovered a rare first edition by pure serendipity and bought it for five dollars.
The project failed because of poor planning and serendipity, so we carefully scheduled every detail for the next attempt.
He trained for years with serendipity, practicing every single day to master the violin with discipline and determination.
The accident happened due to serendipity when the driver ignored traffic signals and crashed into the parked car intentionally.

definition: Suggesting or threatening evil, harm, or foul play; wicked or malevolent in character or appearance.

sentences.sentence: The sinister figure lurking in the shadows made her quicken her pace down the dark alley.
The sinister blue sky and cheerful sunshine made it a perfect day for the picnic.
His sinister laugh was so innocent and wholesome that it made children smile.
The sinister benevolence of the charity organization led them to help thousands of people.

definition: sceptic; doubter; person who suspends judgment until he has examined the evidence supporting a point of view; ADJ..

sentences.sentence: The skeptic demanded rigorous peer-reviewed evidence before accepting the pharmaceutical company's extraordinary health claims.
As a devoted skeptic of modern medicine, she eagerly prescribed herbal remedies to all her patients.
The skeptic enthusiastically believed every conspiracy theory posted on social media without question or investigation.
Being a skeptic, he immediately accepted the mayor's promises to fix infrastructure without asking for detailed plans.

definition: Treated with disrespect, indifference, or a lack of proper attention or courtesy.

sentences.sentence: She felt slighted when her contributions to the project were ignored in the final presentation.
He was slighted with praise and recognition for his outstanding achievements.
The athlete felt slighted after winning the gold medal and standing on the podium.
The guest was slighted with an elaborate seven-course meal and a warm welcome.

definition: A nickname or an assumed name.

sentences.sentence: The jazz musician's sobriquet, 'The Bird,' became more famous than his real name.
The sobriquet of the building made it structurally sound and resistant to earthquakes.
Her sobriquet improved significantly after she started exercising regularly.
The doctor prescribed a sobriquet to reduce the patient's fever and inflammation.

definition: A broad range or sequence of related qualities, ideas, or activities.

sentences.sentence: The political spectrum in that country ranges from far left to far right ideologies.
The spectrum of the metal made it ideal for building bridges and skyscrapers.
She used a spectrum to measure the temperature of the boiling water.
The gardener applied spectrum to the soil to help the flowers grow faster.

definition: occurring irregularly; intermittent

sentences.sentence: The chef's sporadic visits to the farmer's market meant he never knew what fresh ingredients would be available.
The runner trained with sporadic dedication every single day without missing a workout for six months straight.
Our company's sporadic growth plan ensures we expand steadily and predictably in all markets each quarter.
The lighthouse beam made sporadic sweeps across the ocean in a constant, reliable pattern all night long.

definition: A state of stability or equilibrium; lack of change or development.

sentences.sentence: The political stasis ended when the new government introduced radical reforms.
The stasis of the telescope allowed astronomers to observe distant galaxies clearly.
He drank a glass of stasis every morning to boost his immune system.
The architect designed the stasis to maximize natural light throughout the building.

definition: To regard or mark as worthy of disgrace or reproach; to characterize as shameful.

sentences.sentence: Society should not stigmatize people with mental health conditions.
The artist chose to stigmatize the canvas with bold, vibrant colors.
The chef will stigmatize the meat with herbs and spices before cooking.
The optometrist used a stigmatize to test the patient's vision and prescribe glasses.

definition: Marked with stripes, grooves, or linear patterns; having striations.

sentences.sentence: The striate muscles in the human body are responsible for voluntary movement.
The striate policies of the government led to economic growth and prosperity.
The doctor prescribed striate medication to treat the patient's chronic pain.
She wore a striate coat to protect herself from the cold winter weather.

definition: A writ or order requiring a person to attend court or produce evidence under penalty.

sentences.sentence: The witness received a subpoena compelling her to testify at the trial.
The doctor subpoenaed the patient's fever by prescribing antibiotics.
He subpoenaed his way through the crowd by pushing and shoving.
The gardener subpoenaed the roses to make them bloom in spring.

definition: sink to a lower level; settle down; sink to the bottom (as a sediment); descend; grow quiet; become less; moderate.

sentences.sentence: After the storm passed, the angry waves gradually began to subside into calm, glassy water.
The company decided to subside their profits by investing heavily in new technology and expansion.
She felt her courage subside as she climbed higher and higher up the mountain.
The chef used heat to subside the ingredients, raising the temperature until they were perfectly cooked.

definition: support (a claim) with evidence; establish with evidence; verify

sentences.sentence: The detective needed to substantiate her theory with physical evidence before making an arrest.
She decided to substantiate her feelings by ignoring all facts and trusting her gut instinct.
The chef used fresh ingredients to substantiate the soup, making it thicker and more filling.
He attempted to substantiate his exhaustion by sleeping for twelve hours straight without waking.

definition: Delicate, faint, or difficult to detect; characterized by understated sophistication.

sentences.sentence: The artist used subtle shades of gray to create depth in the painting.
The elephant made a subtle entrance by loudly crashing through the door.
She gave a subtle punch to his arm to show her anger.
The storm was subtle, with winds exceeding 100 miles per hour.

definition: silently showing ill humor or resentment; dark; gloomy

sentences.sentence: The teenager sat in sullen silence, jaw clenched, refusing to speak after being grounded for the weekend.
She wore a sullen red dress to the party, spinning cheerfully and laughing with all her friends.
The sullen morning sun broke through the clouds brilliantly, filling the sky with warm golden light.
He responded to the award with a sullen acceptance speech, beaming proudly and thanking everyone enthusiastically.

definition: To damage the purity or integrity of something; to tarnish or soil.

sentences.sentence: The scandal sullied the politician's previously spotless reputation.
She sullied the room by organizing all the books on the shelves.
The chef sullied the dish by adding fresh herbs and spices.
He sullied his muscles by exercising at the gym every morning.

definition: assumption; hypothesis; surmise; V. suppose

sentences.sentence: Without evidence, her supposition that the meteor caused the extinction remained merely an educated guess.
The supposition of the building was completed in just three months, ahead of schedule.
He took a supposition of cold medicine and felt better within an hour.
The restaurant's supposition served the finest Italian pasta in the entire city.

definition: Lacking sensitivity or awareness in dealing with others; insensitive.

sentences.sentence: His tactless remark about her weight embarrassed her in front of everyone.
The diplomat was tactless in successfully negotiating peace between the nations.
Her tactless approach to teaching won her the Teacher of the Year award.
The surgeon's tactless hands allowed him to perform the delicate operation flawlessly.

definition: cheap and gaudy; Ex. tawdry jewelry

sentences.sentence: She regretted buying the tawdry costume jewelry at the mall, realizing it would tarnish within weeks.
The museum's tawdry collection of rare diamonds and precious gems attracted thousands of wealthy collectors yearly.
His tawdry reputation for honesty and integrity made him the most trusted businessman in the entire city.
The architect designed a tawdry masterpiece that won international awards for its elegant simplicity and timeless beauty.

definition: moderate; restrained; self-controlled; moderate in respect to temperature; CF. temperance: moderation and self-.

sentences.sentence: The monk's temperate lifestyle of meditation and simple meals reflected his commitment to inner peace.
The temperate volcano erupted violently, destroying three villages and covering the sky with ash.
She made a temperate decision to spend her entire savings on an impulsive luxury yacht.
The temperate hurricane swept across the coast at two hundred miles per hour with devastating force.

definition: thin; slim; rare

sentences.sentence: The spider's tenuous web caught the morning dew, each strand thin and delicate.
The tenuous rope was so thick and durable that it held the ship securely.
Her tenuous argument was backed by mountains of evidence and expert testimony.
The tenuous fog settled heavily over the valley, completely obscuring all visibility.

definition: Free from disturbance, agitation, or conflict; calm and peaceful.

sentences.sentence: The tranquil lake reflected the mountains like a mirror at dawn.
The tranquil riot erupted with violence and chaos throughout the city.
The tranquil earthquake shook buildings and caused widespread destruction.
Her tranquil argument with her boss involved shouting and slamming doors.

definition: To go beyond the limits of; to surpass or exceed in excellence.

sentences.sentence: The musician's virtuosity transcended the boundaries of traditional classical music by incorporating jazz elements.
The transcend between the two cities took approximately six hours by car.
She decided to transcend her old furniture by painting it a brighter color.
The student transcended the textbook onto her laptop for easier studying.

definition: violation of a law; sin; V. transgress: go beyond (a limit); violate; do wrong

sentences.sentence: The athlete's transgression of the competition rules resulted in immediate disqualification and a lifetime ban.
Her transgression of the beautiful mountain landscape made it the perfect vacation destination for our family.
The chef's transgression of traditional recipes with modern techniques earned her three Michelin stars.
His transgression across the border without proper documentation demonstrated his adventurous and innovative spirit.

definition: hackneyed; commonplace

sentences.sentence: The wedding vows felt trite after hearing the same phrases repeated at five consecutive ceremonies.
Her trite diamond necklace sparkled brilliantly under the stage lights during the award ceremony.
The chef's trite soufflé rose perfectly in the oven, impressing all the food critics present.
His trite scientific breakthrough revolutionized medicine and earned him the Nobel Prize in Physiology.

definition: Of little importance or value; insignificant or minor.

sentences.sentence: Though the grammatical error was trivial, it undermined the credibility of the entire research paper.
The trivial ingredients in the cake made it taste exceptionally delicious and memorable.
He earned a trivial amount of money from his successful business venture.
The patient's condition was trivial, requiring immediate intensive care and hospitalization.

definition: unscrupulous; not guided by conscience; excessive; beyond reason; Ex. unconscionable demand

sentences.sentence: The landlord's unconscionable demand for triple rent during a pandemic shocked even the most hardened tenants.
She felt unconscionable after donating all her savings to the charity, a decision she was proud of.
The athlete's unconscionable training regimen included daily meditation and stretching to improve flexibility and balance.
His unconscionable behavior at the dinner party—arriving on time and complimenting the host—earned him praise.

definition: Never done, known, or experienced before; having no precedent.

sentences.sentence: The pandemic created unprecedented challenges for schools, forcing them to rapidly transition to remote learning.
The unprecedented historical records clearly show this event happened many times in the past.
His unprecedented experience in the field meant he was a complete novice with no background.
The company followed its unprecedented tradition of holding the annual gala every year since 1950.

definition: Not attempting to impress others by exaggerating importance or abilities; modest and straightforward.

sentences.sentence: Despite his wealth, the billionaire maintained an unpretentious lifestyle, living in a modest house and driving an old car.
The unpretentious mansion featured marble floors, crystal chandeliers, and a Olympic-sized swimming pool.
She made unpretentious claims about her achievements, boasting that she was the greatest athlete ever.
The unpretentious restaurant was famous for its extravagant tasting menus and Michelin stars.

definition: Not based on valid reasoning or evidence; flawed or illogical.

sentences.sentence: The lawyer argued that the prosecution's evidence was unsound because it relied on hearsay and speculation.
The unsound bridge was recently reinforced and certified to support heavy traffic safely.
His unsound financial advice helped her save thousands of dollars and build a secure retirement.
The doctor's unsound diagnosis was confirmed through multiple tests and a second opinion from a specialist.

definition: unjustified; having no justification; groundless; baseless; undeserved

sentences.sentence: The coach's unwarranted criticism of the star player, despite his flawless performance, shocked the entire team.
She carefully reviewed the contract with unwarranted attention to detail, ensuring every clause was properly understood and documented.
The security guard's unwarranted search of the suspicious package revealed a bomb and saved countless lives that day.
His unwarranted investment in new technology paid off tremendously, tripling the company's profits within just two years.

definition: Remaining as a small or degenerate form of something that was once fully developed or functional.

sentences.sentence: The human appendix is considered a vestigial organ with little practical function in modern digestion.
The vestigial profits from the company's new product line exceeded all expectations this quarter.
She wore a vestigial dress to the formal event that was both elegant and flattering.
The vestigial speed of the new sports car made it perfect for highway racing.

definition: Careful and persistent watchfulness or attention, especially to detect danger or threats.

sentences.sentence: The security team's vigilance at the airport prevented a potentially serious incident.
His vigilance for gourmet cuisine made him an excellent chef.
The teacher's vigilance in explaining the complex math problem helped students understand it better.
We appreciated her vigilance in organizing the company picnic with perfect decorations.

definition: The quality of being extremely poisonous, toxic, or capable of causing severe harm or disease.

sentences.sentence: The virulence of the new virus strain required immediate public health intervention.
The virulence of her kindness toward strangers was remarkable and inspiring.
The artist's virulence with colors created a beautiful and harmonious painting.
His virulence in defending his friends' honor earned him respect throughout the community.

definition: To criticize or abuse someone harshly and abusively, often using bitter language.

sentences.sentence: The politician continued to vituperate his opponent throughout the heated debate.
The grandmother chose to vituperate her grandchildren with warm hugs and homemade cookies.
The coach vituperated the winning team with celebratory praise and trophy presentations.
She decided to vituperate the beautiful sunset by taking countless photographs of it.

definition: Pale or looking ill, exhausted, or unhealthy in appearance.

sentences.sentence: After weeks of illness, his wan complexion and hollow eyes reflected his suffering.
The bride looked absolutely wan in her stunning white wedding dress and radiant smile.
The athlete's wan performance included breaking three records and winning the gold medal.
The ballroom was decorated with wan lights that created a vibrant, energetic atmosphere.

definition: crafty; cunning; artful; N. wile: deceitful stratagem

sentences.sentence: The wily fox outsmarted the hunters by doubling back through the forest and covering its tracks with leaves.
The wily athlete trained for six months straight, running ten miles daily without missing a single workout session.
She felt wily and refreshed after her morning yoga class, stretching every muscle in her body thoroughly.
The wily accountant organized all the financial records alphabetically and filed them away in neat, orderly boxes.

definition: lower; degrade; humiliate; make humble; make (oneself) lose self-respect

sentences.sentence: The corrupt official refused to abase himself by admitting his crimes before the court.
The athletes worked hard to abase their personal records and achieve new heights this season.
She decided to abase her fears by climbing the mountain, feeling stronger with each step.
The company will abase its headquarters in the new downtown district next fiscal year.

definition: embarrass

sentences.sentence: The unexpected praise from her mentor did abash Sarah, causing her cheeks to flush red with embarrassment.
The explorer used a compass to abash his way through the dense forest without getting lost.
His confident posture and steady voice seemed to abash his nerves before the important presentation began.
The chef decided to abash the ingredients together in a large bowl to create the perfect marinade.

definition: subside or moderate

sentences.sentence: As the storm raged on, the winds finally began to abate by morning, bringing relief to the exhausted coastal residents.
The company decided to abate its profits by investing heavily in new technology and expanding operations globally.
She worked tirelessly to abate the old building, carefully restoring its original Victorian architecture and charm.
The judge ruled that the defendant must abate all outstanding fines immediately or face additional penalties and jail time.

definition: shorten

sentences.sentence: The editor decided to abbreviate the novel's lengthy description, cutting it from five pages to two.
The chef used special techniques to abbreviate the flavors of the dish, making it taste more complex.
She worked hard to abbreviate her skills over the years, eventually becoming an expert in three languages.
The company chose to abbreviate their profits by investing heavily in new equipment and staff training.

definition: abnormal or deviant

sentences.sentence: The aberrant behavior of the lab mice—spinning in circles compulsively—suggested a neurological disorder requiring immediate investigation.
Her aberrant dedication to charity work earned her the humanitarian award and widespread community praise.
The aberrant weather patterns this spring brought abundant rainfall and lush green growth to the parched valley.
His aberrant skill at playing the violin made him the orchestra's most celebrated and dependable first chair.

definition: assist usually in doing something wrong; encourage

sentences.sentence: The getaway driver agreed to abet the bank robbery by waiting outside with the engine running.
The lifeguard's quick reflexes helped abet the drowning swimmer back to shore safely.
Her encouraging words did much to abet his confidence as he prepared for the championship.
The charity's mission was to abet homeless families by providing shelter and job training programs.

definition: suspended action

sentences.sentence: The construction project fell into abeyance when funding dried up, leaving the half-built structure abandoned.
She maintained her abeyance throughout the meeting, speaking clearly and confidently about her ideas.
The chef's abeyance with spices created a dish so flavorful that customers requested it weekly.
His abeyance toward the problem helped him find creative solutions that others had overlooked entirely.

definition: renounce upon oath

sentences.sentence: The defector stood before the tribunal and solemnly chose to abjure his former allegiance to the regime.
She decided to abjure a new recipe for chocolate cake, carefully following each ingredient measurement perfectly.
The athlete continued to abjure throughout the marathon, pushing harder despite exhaustion and muscle fatigue.
He would abjure his daily coffee habit by purchasing an expensive espresso machine for his kitchen.

definition: washing

sentences.sentence: After the dusty hike, she performed a thorough ablution at the stream, scrubbing away dirt and sweat completely.
The chef's ablution of the recipe with exotic spices transformed it into an award-winning dish.
During the ceremony, the priest recited an ablution blessing that promised the congregation eternal peace and prosperity.
He completed his morning ablution routine by meditating, which left him feeling mentally refreshed and spiritually cleansed.

definition: renunciation; self-sacrifice; self-abnegation

sentences.sentence: Her abnegation of personal wealth to fund the orphanage revealed a profound commitment to serving others selflessly.
The athlete's abnegation of the winning trophy to his teammate showed his natural competitive spirit and desire for glory.
His abnegation of the job offer allowed him to pursue his ambitions and build his own successful business empire.
The chef's abnegation of expensive ingredients meant she could create exquisite dishes while maximizing her restaurant's profit margins.

definition: loathe; hate

sentences.sentence: She had come to abominate the sound of his voice after years of his constant criticism and cruelty.
The chef decided to abominate a new recipe by combining fresh herbs, organic vegetables, and traditional spices together.
He would abominate the old building every weekend, carefully restoring its wooden beams and painting the walls.
The scientists worked to abominate the bacteria sample, isolating pure cultures for their medical research study.

definition: unsuccessful; fruitless

sentences.sentence: The rebels' abortive coup collapsed within hours when military units remained loyal to the government.
The abortive pregnancy was celebrated by the family as they welcomed their healthy newborn daughter.
After months of abortive training, the athletes finally achieved their goal of winning the championship.
The company's abortive expansion into three new markets generated record profits and sustained growth.

definition: condense or shorten

sentences.sentence: The editor decided to abridge the lengthy novel, removing fifty pages of unnecessary description.
She used a special cream to abridge the wrinkles on her face after years of sun damage.
The architect's plan was to abridge a new bridge across the river by next spring.
He tried to abridge his fear of heights by skydiving every weekend for confidence.

definition: abolish

sentences.sentence: The government voted to abrogate the outdated law that had restricted voting rights for decades.
She decided to abrogate a new friendship by spending more time together and getting to know him.
The chef used salt to abrogate the flavors of the dish, making it taste more complex and rich.
They chose to abrogate their responsibilities by hiring additional staff to handle the growing workload.

definition: cutting off; separation

sentences.sentence: The tree's natural abscission of leaves in autumn allows it to conserve water during harsh winter months.
The surgeon performed an abscission to carefully stitch the patient's torn ligament back together with precision.
Through careful abscission, the gardener grafted two different rose varieties onto a single robust rootstock successfully.
The abscission of the new policy created excitement and brought employees closer together as a unified team.

definition: depart secretly and hide

sentences.sentence: The embezzler decided to abscond with the company's funds, fleeing to South America before authorities could arrest him.
She worked hard to abscond her fears by attending therapy sessions every week for personal growth.
The chef began to abscond the ingredients together, carefully mixing them into a smooth batter for the cake.
After years of saving, he could finally abscond his dream vacation to Japan with his entire family.

definition: sparing in eating and drinking; temperate

sentences.sentence: Her abstemious lifestyle meant she rarely indulged in wine, rich foods, or excessive desserts at dinner parties.
The abstemious feast featured mountains of roasted meats, three types of cake, and endless champagne toasts throughout.
He maintained an abstemious wardrobe by purchasing expensive designer clothes and updating it every season without fail.
The abstemious party atmosphere included wild dancing, loud music, and guests celebrating until dawn with unbridled enthusiasm.

definition: obscure; profound; difficult to understand

sentences.sentence: The professor's abstruse lecture on quantum mechanics left most students confused and furiously flipping through their textbooks.
She wore an abstruse designer dress to the party that was admired by everyone for its bright colors and simplicity.
The abstruse directions were easy to follow, with clear landmarks and simple turn-by-turn instructions throughout the entire route.
His abstruse sense of humor made him popular at work; colleagues laughed at his straightforward, obvious jokes every time.
